calculator.description: Calculate the area of the sector using the radius and angle.

steps.title: Identify radius and angle
steps.content:
First, we need the radius of the circle. We are given the diameter, so we divide it by 2. From the previous part of the question, we also know the angle subtended is $\theta = \frac{2\pi}{3}$ radians.

$$\begin{aligned} r &= \frac{\text{diameter}}{2} \\ r &= \frac{20}{2} = 10 \text{ cm} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Convert radians to degrees
steps.content:
To use the proportional method, we need our angle in degrees. Since $\pi \text{ radians} = 180^{\circ}$, we multiply the radian value by $\frac{180}{\pi}$:

$$\theta_{\text{deg}} = \frac{2\pi}{3} \times \frac{180}{\pi} = \frac{360}{3} = 120^{\circ}$$

steps.title: Apply proportional area method
steps.content:
The area of a sector is a fraction of the total area of the circle. This fraction is determined by the ratio of the sector's angle to the full $360^{\circ}$ of a circle. 

$$\text{Area} = \frac{\theta_{\text{deg}}}{360} \times \pi r^2$$

Substituting our values ($r = 10$ and $\theta_{\text{deg}} = 120^{\circ}$):

$$\text{Area} = \frac{120}{360} \times \pi(10)^2$$

steps.title: Simplify and solve
steps.content:
Now we simplify the expression. The fraction $\frac{120}{360}$ reduces to $\frac{1}{3}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Area} &= \frac{1}{3} \times 100\pi \\ \text{Area} &= \frac{100\pi}{3} \text{ cm}^2 \end{aligned}$$

According to the markscheme, this exact form is expected for full marks.

parts.content: If the angle $\frac{2\pi}{3}$ is doubled, what would be the new area of the sector?
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate new angle: $\frac{2\pi}{3} \times 2 = \frac{4\pi}{3}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Use sector area formula: $A = \frac{1}{2}r^2\theta$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute values: $A = \frac{1}{2} \times 10^2 \times \frac{4\pi}{3}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the area: $A = \frac{200\pi}{3} \text{ cm}^2$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Find the radius
steps.content:
First, we need the radius of the circle. Since the diameter is given as $20 \text{ cm}$, the radius $r$ is simply half of that value:

$$r = \frac{20}{2} = 10 \text{ cm}$$

steps.title: Calculate the new angle
steps.content:
The question states that the original angle of $\frac{2\pi}{3}$ is doubled. To find the new angle $\theta$, we multiply the original angle by $2$:

$$\theta = \frac{2\pi}{3} \times 2 = \frac{4\pi}{3}$$

steps.title: Use the sector formula
steps.content:
To find the area of the sector when the angle is in radians, we use the formula found in the **Geometry and Trigonometry** section of your data booklet:

$$A = \frac{1}{2} r^2 \theta$$

steps.title: Substitute and calculate area
steps.content:
Now, substitute $r = 10$ and $\theta = \frac{4\pi}{3}$ into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} A &= \frac{1}{2} \times (10)^2 \times \frac{4\pi}{3} \\ A &= \frac{1}{2} \times 100 \times \frac{4\pi}{3} \\ A &= 50 \times \frac{4\pi}{3} \end{aligned}$$

Simplifying this gives the final exact area:

$$A = \frac{200\pi}{3} \text{ cm}^2$$

parts.content: Calculate the linear speed of a point on the edge of the wheel.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify correct formula for linear speed: $v = r\omega$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute values correctly: $v = 25 \times \frac{\pi}{120}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate final answer: $v = \frac{5\pi}{24}$ metres per second <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Accept equivalent forms of the answer if correctly derived</note>

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
To find the linear speed using the distance-time approach, we first need to identify the radius of the wheel and the time it takes for one full trip around the circle (the period).

From the question, we have:
- Radius $r = 25$ m
- Period $T = 4$ minutes

steps.title: Calculate the circumference
steps.content:
Linear speed is the distance traveled divided by the time taken. For one complete revolution, the distance traveled is the circumference of the Ferris wheel.

Using the formula for the circumference of a circle (covered in **Topic 3.4: Geometry and Trigonometry**):

$$\text{Distance} = 2\pi r$$

Substituting our radius:

$$\text{Distance} = 2 \times \pi \times 25 = 50\pi \text{ metres}$$

steps.title: Convert time to seconds
steps.content:
Speed is typically measured in metres per second ($m/s$). Since our period is currently in minutes, we need to convert it to seconds.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Time} &= 4 \text{ minutes} \times 60 \text{ seconds/minute} \\ \text{Time} &= 240 \text{ seconds} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 4 * 60 on the home screen.

calculator.description: Convert 4 minutes to seconds.

steps.title: Calculate linear speed
steps.content:
Now we apply the basic speed formula: $v = \frac{d}{t}$.

$$\begin{aligned} v &= \frac{50\pi}{240} \end{aligned}$$

To simplify the fraction, we divide both the numerator and denominator by 10:

$$\begin{aligned} v &= \frac{5\pi}{24} \text{ metres per second} \end{aligned}$$

This matches the final answer required by the markscheme!

gdc.instructions: In the Run-Matrix menu, enter 50 / 240 and use the F-D key to view as a fraction.

ti84.instructions: Enter 50 / 240. To convert to a fraction, press [MATH] [ENTER] [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type 50 / 240 and click the fraction icon next to the result.

calculator.description: Simplify the fraction 50/240.

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
To calculate the linear speed, we first need to extract the key values from the problem. We are given the radius of the Ferris wheel and the time it takes to complete one full turn (the period).

*   **Radius ($r$):** $25$ metres
*   **Period ($T$):** $4$ minutes

Since speed is usually expressed in metres per second (m/s) in IB mathematics, our first task will be to convert the time into seconds.

steps.title: Convert time to seconds
steps.content:
There are $60$ seconds in one minute. To find the total time in seconds for one revolution, we multiply the number of minutes by $60$:

$$T = 4 \times 60 = 240 \text{ seconds}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 4 * 60 on the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 4 * 60 into a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the total number of seconds in 4 minutes.

steps.title: Calculate angular velocity (ω)
steps.content:
Angular velocity ($\omega$) represents the angle (in radians) covered per unit of time. A full revolution is $2\pi$ radians. The formula for angular velocity is:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

Substituting our period $T = 240$ seconds:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{240} = \frac{\pi}{120} \text{ rad/s}$$

steps.title: Calculate linear speed (v)
steps.content:
Now we can use the relationship between linear speed ($v$) and angular velocity ($\omega$):

$$v = r\omega$$

Substituting our values $r = 25$ and $\omega = \frac{\pi}{120}$:

$$\begin{aligned} v &= 25 \times \frac{\pi}{120} \\ v &= \frac{25\pi}{120} \end{aligned}$$

Simplifying the fraction by dividing both the numerator and denominator by $5$:

$$v = \frac{5\pi}{24} \text{ metres per second}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide 25 by 120 and simplify the resulting fraction.

ti84.instructions: Enter 25 * (pi / 120) and press ENTER. To simplify the fraction, enter 25/120 and press MATH -> 1:Frac.

desmos.instructions: Type 25 * (pi / 120) to get the decimal result, or 25 / 120 and click the fraction icon to simplify.

calculator.description: Calculate the decimal value of the linear speed and simplify the fraction.

steps.title: Final answer and marks
steps.content:
The linear speed of a point on the edge of the wheel is:

$$v = \frac{5\pi}{24} \approx 0.654 \text{ m/s}$$

According to the markscheme, you earn:
*   **M1** for identifying the correct formula $v = r\omega$.
*   **M1** for the correct substitution of values.
*   **A1** for the final simplified answer.

parts.content: How far does a point on the edge travel in 10 minutes?
parts.markscheme:
- Recognize relationship between distance, speed, and time: $d = v \times t$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Convert time to seconds: $t = 10 \times 60 = 600$ seconds <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate total distance: $d = \frac{5\pi}{24} \times 600 = 125\pi$ metres <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the motion
steps.content: To find how far the point travels, we first need to understand how many times the Ferris wheel goes around in the given time. We are told the wheel makes **one complete revolution every 4 minutes**, and we want to know the distance traveled in **10 minutes**.

steps.title: Calculate number of revolutions
steps.content:
First, let's find the total number of revolutions by dividing the total time by the time it takes for one revolution (the period):

$$\text{Number of revolutions} = \frac{10 \text{ minutes}}{4 \text{ minutes}} = 2.5 \text{ revolutions}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 10 / 4 on the home screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of revolutions by dividing the total time by the period.

steps.title: Find circumference of wheel
steps.content:
The distance traveled in one full revolution is equal to the circumference of the circle. Using the radius $r = 25\text{ m}$, we use the formula for circumference:

$$\begin{aligned} C &= 2\pi r \\ C &= 2 \times \pi \times 25 \\ C &= 50\pi \text{ metres} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 2 * 25.

calculator.description: Calculate the circumference coefficient by multiplying 2 and 25.

steps.title: Calculate total distance
steps.content:
Finally, we multiply the total number of revolutions by the distance of one revolution (the circumference) to get the total distance $d$:

$$\begin{aligned} d &= 2.5 \times 50\pi \\ d &= 125\pi \text{ metres} \end{aligned}$$

If you need a decimal approximation, $125\pi \approx 393 \text{ m}$ (to 3 significant figures).

ti84.instructions: Enter 2.5 * 50.

calculator.description: Multiply the number of revolutions by the circumference coefficient.

steps.title: Identify the travel time
steps.content:
First, let's identify the total time the Ferris wheel is moving. The question asks for the distance traveled in 10 minutes. Since standard physics units and the markscheme often use seconds, we will convert this time now.

$$\begin{aligned} t &= 10 \times 60 \\ t &= 600 \text{ seconds} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: Convert time to seconds: t = 10 \times 60 = 600 seconds

steps.title: Calculate the linear speed
steps.content:
The linear speed ($v$) is the distance traveled in one revolution (the circumference) divided by the time it takes for one revolution.

1.  **Find the Circumference:** Using the formula $C = 2\pi r$, where the radius is 25 metres:
    $$C = 2 \times \pi \times 25 = 50\pi \text{ metres}$$
2.  **Find the Time for one revolution:** The wheel makes one complete revolution every 4 minutes, which is 240 seconds ($4 \times 60$).
3.  **Calculate Speed ($v$):**
    $$\begin{aligned} v &= \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Time}} \\ v &= \frac{50\pi}{240} \\ v &= \frac{5\pi}{24} \text{ metres per second} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate total distance
steps.content:
Now that we have the linear speed and the total travel time ($t = 600$ seconds), we can find the total distance ($d$) using the formula:

$$d = v \times t$$

Substituting our values into the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} d &= \frac{5\pi}{24} \times 600 \\ d &= \frac{3000\pi}{24} \\ d &= 125\pi \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Type the expression using the pi symbol (2nd + ^).

desmos.instructions: Enter the linear speed expression multiplied by the total time.

calculator.description: Calculate the final distance by multiplying the linear velocity by the total time.

highlights.text: Recognize relationship between distance, speed, and time: d = v \times t

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The point on the edge travels $125\pi$ metres. In an IB exam, you can leave your answer in terms of $\pi$ for exactness, or provide a decimal approximation rounded to 3 significant figures.

$$d = 125\pi \approx 393 \text{ metres}$$

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
To find the distance traveled, we first need to identify the radius and how long the Ferris wheel has been turning. From the question, we know:

*   The radius $r = 25$ m
*   One revolution takes 4 minutes
*   Total time $t = 10$ minutes

steps.title: Calculate angular displacement
steps.content:
Since the Ferris wheel makes one full revolution in 4 minutes, we can find the total angle $\theta$ (in radians) covered in 10 minutes. 

One full revolution is equal to $2\pi$ radians. 

$$\text{Revolutions} = \frac{10 \text{ minutes}}{4 \text{ minutes}} = 2.5 \text{ revolutions}$$

Now, convert this to radians:
$$\theta = 2.5 \times 2\pi = 5\pi \text{ radians}$$

steps.title: Apply arc length formula
steps.content:
The distance traveled by a point on the edge of a circle is the arc length $s$. We use the formula from the data booklet:

$$s = r\theta$$

Substituting our values $r = 25$ and $\theta = 5\pi$:

$$\begin{aligned} s &= 25 \times 5\pi \\ s &= 125\pi \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: Calculating the arc length given a radius and specific angle

steps.title: Calculate numerical value
steps.content: Let's use a calculator to find the final numerical value. While the exact answer is $125\pi$, we usually provide a decimal rounded to 3 significant figures.

ti84.instructions: Enter 125 * π and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 125 * pi in a new cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the total distance using 125 * pi.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content: The total distance traveled by the point is $125\pi$ metres, which is approximately $393$ metres (to 3 significant figures).

parts.content: Express $210^\circ$ in radians.
parts.markscheme:
- Using formula $\theta = \frac{\pi}{180} \times 210$ <marks>M1</marks>

- $\theta = \frac{7\pi}{6}$ radians <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<examTip>Remember to include 'radians' in your final answer</examTip>

steps.title: Identify the conversion factor
steps.content:
To convert an angle from degrees to radians, we use the relationship that $180^\circ$ is equal to $\pi$ radians. 

The conversion factor we use is:
$$\text{Radians} = \text{Degrees} \times \frac{\pi}{180}$$

This concept is a key part of **Topic 3: Geometry and Trigonometry (AHL 3.7 Radians)**. Knowing how to switch between these units is essential for working with circular motion and sectors.

steps.title: Apply the conversion formula
steps.content:
We take the given angle of $210^\circ$ and multiply it by the conversion factor $\frac{\pi}{180}$:

$$\theta = 210 \times \frac{\pi}{180}$$

Setting up this calculation correctly earns you the first method mark (**M1**).

steps.title: Simplify the fraction
steps.content:
Now, we simplify the fraction $\frac{210}{180}$ to its lowest terms. 

$$\begin{aligned} \theta &= \frac{210\pi}{180} \\ \theta &= \frac{21\pi}{18} \quad \text{(dividing by 10)} \\ \theta &= \frac{7\pi}{6} \quad \text{(dividing by 3)} \end{aligned}$$

Both numbers are divisible by $30$, giving us the final simplified result. This final step earns you the accuracy mark (**A1**).

gdc.instructions: Enter 210 / 180. Most GDCs will automatically simplify the fraction or provide a 'convert to fraction' button.

ti84.instructions: Enter 210 / 180 and press ENTER. To convert to a fraction, press MATH, select 1: >Frac, and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 210/180. Click the small 'fraction' icon to the left of the result to see 7/6.

calculator.description: Simplify the fraction 210/180 on your calculator.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The angle in radians is:
$$\theta = \frac{7\pi}{6} \text{ radians}$$

Don't forget to include the units 'radians' as suggested in the exam tip!

highlights.text: Remember to include 'radians' in your final answer

steps.title: Establish the relationship
steps.content:
To convert degrees to radians using proportional reasoning, we first recall the fundamental relationship between the two units. A straight angle (half a circle) is defined as $180^\circ$, which is exactly $\pi$ radians.

This gives us our reference ratio:
$$180^\circ = \pi \text{ radians}$$ 

This concept is a prerequisite for **Topic 3: Geometry and Trigonometry**, specifically **AHL 3.7 Radians**.

steps.title: Set up the proportion
steps.content:
We can set up a proportion where the ratio of the given angle in degrees to $180^\circ$ is equal to the ratio of the unknown angle in radians ($x$) to $\pi$ radians.

$$\frac{210}{180} = \frac{x}{\pi}$$

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.content:
To isolate $x$, we multiply both sides of the equation by $\pi$:

$$\begin{aligned} x &= \frac{210}{180} \times \pi \\ x &= \frac{210\pi}{180} \end{aligned}$$

In the IB markscheme, identifying this relationship or setting up the calculation correctly earns you the first mark (**M1**).

steps.title: Simplify and state answer
steps.content:
Now, simplify the fraction $\frac{210}{180}$ by dividing both the numerator and denominator by their greatest common divisor, 30:

$$\frac{210 \div 30}{180 \div 30} = \frac{7}{6}$$

So, the angle in radians is:
$$x = \frac{7\pi}{6} \text{ radians}$$

Always remember to include the units 'radians' in your final answer to ensure you get the accuracy mark (**A1**).

gdc.instructions: Enter 210 / 180 and use the fraction conversion tool (usually labeled a b/c or S->D).

ti84.instructions: Enter 210 / 180 and press [MATH] [ENTER] [ENTER] to convert to a simplified fraction.

desmos.instructions: Type 210/180 into a cell and click the 'Convert to Fraction' icon on the left.

calculator.description: Simplify the fraction 210/180 on a calculator.

highlights.text: Remember to include 'radians' in your final answer

steps.title: Identify a common angle
steps.content:
To solve this using the **multiples of common angles** approach, we first look for a standard angle (like $30^\circ$, $45^\circ$, or $60^\circ$) that goes into $210^\circ$ perfectly. 

Since $21$ is a multiple of $3$, we can see that:
$$210^\circ = 7 \times 30^\circ$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 210 / 30 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 210 / 30 into a new expression line.

calculator.description: Verify that 210 divided by 30 is 7.

steps.title: Recall the radian equivalent
steps.content:
Next, we use our knowledge of standard radian measures. A full half-circle ($180^\circ$) is equal to $\pi$ radians. Therefore, $30^\circ$ (which is $180^\circ \div 6$) is equivalent to:
$$30^\circ = \frac{\pi}{6} \text{ radians}$$

This concept is a core part of **Topic 3: Geometry and Trigonometry (AHL 3.7)**. Understanding these common conversions makes trigonometry much faster!

steps.title: Calculate the total radians
steps.content:
Now, we simply substitute our radian value for $30^\circ$ back into our original multiple:

$$\begin{aligned} 210^\circ &= 7 \times 30^\circ \\ &= 7 \times \frac{\pi}{6} \\ &= \frac{7\pi}{6} \end{aligned}$$

In an IB exam, showing the setup $\frac{\pi}{180} \times 210$ or the equivalent logical step earns you the first mark (**M1**).

ti84.instructions: Enter (7 * π) / 6 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the decimal value to verify against the final answer.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The final answer is $\frac{7\pi}{6}$ radians. 

Always remember to include the units in your final statement to ensure you get the accuracy mark (**A1**).

highlights.text: Remember to include 'radians' in your final answer

parts.content: Determine the arc length covered by a point on the rim of the Ferris wheel when it rotates through $\frac{7\pi}{6}$ radians.
parts.markscheme:
- Using arc length formula $s = r\theta$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Substituting $r = 15$ m and $\theta = \frac{7\pi}{6}$ radians <marks>A1</marks>

- $s = 15 \times \frac{7\pi}{6} = \frac{105\pi}{6} = 17.5\pi$ m <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the arc length formula
steps.content:
To find the distance covered by a point on the rim (the arc length), we use the formula for arc length when the angle is given in radians. This formula relates the radius $r$ and the angle $\theta$ directly to the arc length $s$.

$$s = r\theta$$

steps.title: Extract the given values
steps.content:
From the question, we can identify the two values needed for our formula:
- The radius, $r = 15$ m
- The angle of rotation, $\theta = \frac{7\pi}{6}$ radians

steps.title: Substitute and simplify
steps.content:
Substitute the values into the arc length formula:

$$\begin{aligned} s &= 15 \times \frac{7\pi}{6} \\ s &= \frac{105\pi}{6} \end{aligned}$$

Dividing 105 by 6 gives us $17.5$. So, the exact arc length is:

$$s = 17.5\pi \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Using technology for verification
steps.content: While leaving the answer in terms of $\pi$ is often preferred in IB for exactness, you can also use your calculator to find the decimal value to 3 significant figures.

gdc.instructions: Enter 17.5 * π in the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Type 17.5 then [2nd] [^] (which is the PI symbol) and press [ENTER].

calculator.description: Calculate the numerical value of the arc length.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content: The arc length covered is $17.5\pi$ metres, which is approximately $55.0$ m (to 3 significant figures).

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content:
To find the arc length using proportionality, we will first calculate the distance of one full revolution (the circumference) and then determine what fraction of that distance is covered by the given angle.

From the question, we know:
- The radius $r = 15$ m
- The angle of rotation $\theta = \frac{7\pi}{6}$ radians

steps.title: Calculate total circumference
steps.content:
A full revolution covers the entire circumference ($C$) of the Ferris wheel. 

Using the fundamental formula for circumference:
$$C = 2\pi r$$

Substituting our radius:
$$C = 2 \times \pi \times 15 = 30\pi \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Find the rotation fraction
steps.content:
A complete circle is $2\pi$ radians. We can find the proportion of the circle covered by dividing the given angle by a full revolution:

$$\text{Fraction} = \frac{\text{angle}}{2\pi} = \frac{\frac{7\pi}{6}}{2\pi}$$

Simplifying this fraction:
$$\text{Fraction} = \frac{7\pi}{6} \times \frac{1}{2\pi} = \frac{7}{12}$$

steps.title: Calculate the arc length
steps.content:
Finally, we multiply the total circumference by the fraction of the circle covered to find the arc length ($s$):

$$s = 30\pi \times \frac{7}{12}$$

$$s = \frac{210\pi}{12}$$

Dividing 210 by 12 gives us:
$$s = 17.5\pi \text{ m}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 210 / 12 on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type 210 / 12 to find the decimal value.

calculator.description: Calculate the final coefficient 210 / 12.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
The arc length covered is $17.5\pi$ metres. 

Note that this logic leads directly to the arc length formula found in the markscheme: $$s = r\theta = 15 \times \frac{7\pi}{6} = 17.5\pi$$

parts.content: Calculate the area of the sector formed by a rotation of $\frac{7\pi}{6}$ radians.
parts.markscheme:
- State formula $A = \frac{1}{2}r^2\theta$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Substitute values correctly: $r = 15$ and $\theta = \frac{7\pi}{6}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Correct working and final answer:

$$A = \frac{1}{2} \times 15^2 \times \frac{7\pi}{6} = \frac{1575\pi}{12} = \frac{525\pi}{4} \text{ m}^2$$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Find the angular speed of the Ferris wheel in radians per second.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognize that angular speed $\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T}$ where T is the period <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute $T = 90$ seconds and calculate:
$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{90} = \frac{\pi}{45}$ radians per second <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<examTip>Remember to include units (radians per second) in your final answer</examTip>

steps.title: Identify the period
steps.content:
First, we need to identify the time it takes for the Ferris wheel to complete one full cycle. In physics and trigonometry, this is known as the **period** ($T$). 

The question states that the wheel completes one full revolution in 90 seconds, so:
$$T = 90 \text{ s}$$

highlights.text: completes one full revolution in 90 seconds

steps.title: State the angular speed formula
steps.content:
Angular speed ($\omega$) represents how many radians the wheel turns through every second. Since a full revolution is $2\pi$ radians, the formula relating angular speed to the period is:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

This is a fundamental definition you'll use often when working with circular motion or sinusoidal models (like those found in **Topic 2: Functions**).

steps.title: Substitute and simplify
steps.content:
Now, we substitute our value of $T = 90$ into the formula:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{90}$$

To simplify the fraction, we divide both the numerator and the denominator by 2:

$$\omega = \frac{\pi}{45} \text{ radians per second}$$

steps.title: Calculate the decimal value
steps.content: While the exact form $\frac{\pi}{45}$ is often preferred, you can also provide the decimal equivalent. Using your calculator, you can find the value to three significant figures.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [2nd] [^] (for π).
2. Press [÷] [4] [5].
3. Press [ENTER].

calculator.description: Calculate the angular speed value.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
The angular speed of the Ferris wheel is:

$$\omega \approx 0.0698 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$$

Don't forget to include the units as requested in the exam tip!

steps.title: Identify the initial frequency
steps.content: To find the angular speed using the unit conversion approach, we first look at the motion as a rate of revolutions over time. The problem states that the Ferris wheel completes one full turn every 90 seconds.

highlights.text: completes one full revolution in 90 seconds

steps.title: Express motion as a rate
steps.content:
We can write this motion as a frequency:

$$\text{Rate} = \frac{1 \text{ revolution}}{90 \text{ seconds}}$$

steps.title: Apply the conversion factor
steps.content:
The question asks for the speed in **radians per second**. We know that one full revolution is exactly $2\pi$ radians. 

We multiply our initial rate by this conversion factor:

$$\omega = \frac{1 \text{ revolution}}{90 \text{ seconds}} \times \frac{2\pi \text{ radians}}{1 \text{ revolution}}$$

steps.title: Simplify the expression
steps.content:
Now we simplify the fraction. The "revolutions" units cancel out, leaving us with:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{90} \text{ radians per second}$$

Dividing both the numerator and the denominator by 2 gives us the exact value:

$$\omega = \frac{\pi}{45} \text{ rad/s}$$

gdc.instructions:
1. In the calculation menu, type 2/90 to see the simplified fraction 1/45.
2. Calculate 2π / 90 to get the decimal equivalent.

ti84.instructions:
1. Enter 2/90 and press [MATH] then [1: >Frac] to see the simplified fraction 1/45.
2. To find the decimal value, type (2 * π) / 90 and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type '2pi / 90' into the expression bar. Desmos will provide the decimal result and you can click the fraction icon to see the simplified coefficients.

calculator.description: Simplify the fraction or find the decimal value for angular speed.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The angular speed of the Ferris wheel is:

$$\omega = \frac{\pi}{45} \approx 0.0698 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$$

Note: You earn the first mark for recognizing the relationship between the full circle ($2\pi$) and the time (90s), and the second mark for the final simplified answer.

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
First, let's identify the key information from the problem. We are given the radius of the Ferris wheel and the time it takes to complete one full revolution (the period).

- Radius ($r$) = $15$ metres
- Period ($T$) = $90$ seconds

highlights.text: completes one full revolution in 90 seconds

steps.title: Calculate linear velocity
steps.content:
Using the **Linear Velocity Relationship** approach, we first find the linear velocity ($v$) of a point on the rim. This is the distance of one full revolution (the circumference) divided by the time taken ($T$).

From the syllabus section **SL 3.4**, we know the circumference of a circle is $2\pi r$. So:

$$v = \frac{2\pi r}{T}$$

Substituting our values:

$$v = \frac{2\pi(15)}{90} = \frac{30\pi}{90} = \frac{\pi}{3} \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression (2 * π * 15) / 90. The result is approximately 1.047.

ti84.instructions: Type (2 * π * 15) / 90 and press ENTER. To keep it in terms of π, simplify 30/90 manually.

calculator.description: Calculate the linear velocity in terms of pi.

steps.title: Relate linear to angular speed
steps.content:
Now, we use the fundamental relationship between linear velocity ($v$) and angular speed ($\omega$):

$$v = \omega r$$

Rearranging this formula to solve for angular speed gives us:

$$\omega = \frac{v}{r}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final angular speed
steps.content:
Substitute our value for $v$ and $r$ into the equation:

$$\omega = \frac{\frac{\pi}{3}}{15}$$

To divide a fraction by a whole number, we multiply the denominator:

$$\omega = \frac{\pi}{3 \times 15} = \frac{\pi}{45} \text{ radians per second}$$

This matches the markscheme's requirement of recognizing the relationship between the period and the angular speed.

gdc.instructions: Calculate π / 45 to get the decimal value.

ti84.instructions: Type (π / 3) / 15 or π / 45 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Verify the final numerical value of the angular speed.

parts.content: Calculate the linear speed of a point on the rim of the Ferris wheel.
parts.markscheme:
- Use formula $v = r\omega$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Substitute $r = 15$ and $\omega = \frac{\pi}{45}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Solve correctly:

$$v = 15 \times \frac{\pi}{45} = \frac{15\pi}{45} = \frac{\pi}{3} \text{ m/s}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify known variables
steps.content:
To find the linear speed using the circumference and period, we first need to extract the radius and the time for one full lap (the period) from the question.

*   Radius ($r$) = $15 \text{ m}$
*   Period ($T$) = $90 \text{ s}$

highlights.text: completes one full revolution in 90 seconds

steps.title: Calculate the circumference
steps.content:
The linear speed is the distance traveled per unit of time. In one full revolution, the point on the rim travels a distance equal to the circumference ($C$) of the circle. We use the fundamental formula for circumference:

$$C = 2\pi r$$

Substituting our radius:

$$C = 2 \times \pi \times 15 = 30\pi \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Calculate the linear speed
steps.content:
The linear speed ($v$) is calculated by dividing the total distance traveled in one revolution by the time it took to complete that revolution (the period, $T$):

$$v = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Time}} = \frac{C}{T}$$

Substituting our values:

$$v = \frac{30\pi}{90}$$

Dividing both the numerator and denominator by 30, we get the exact value:

$$v = \frac{\pi}{3} \text{ m/s}$$

steps.title: Verify with technology
steps.content: While $\frac{\pi}{3}$ is the exact answer, you can also provide a decimal approximation. To 3 significant figures, this is approximately $1.05 \text{ m/s}$.

2. Press [execute].

2. Type [ / ] 3 and press [enter].

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.content:
To find the linear speed using the angular velocity approach, we first need to identify the radius ($r$) and the time it takes for one full revolution, known as the period ($T$).

From the question, we have:
- Radius: $r = 15 \text{ m}$
- Period: $T = 90 \text{ s}$

steps.title: Calculate angular velocity
steps.content:
Angular velocity ($\omega$) represents the angle turned per unit of time. Since one full revolution is $2\pi$ radians, the formula is:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

Substituting our values:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{90} = \frac{\pi}{45} \text{ rad/s}$$

steps.title: Apply linear speed formula
steps.content:
Now, we relate the angular velocity to the linear speed ($v$) using the relationship between radius and circular motion:

$$v = r\omega$$

Substituting $r = 15$ and $\omega = \frac{\pi}{45}$:

$$v = 15 \times \frac{\pi}{45}$$

steps.title: Calculate final speed
steps.content:
Simplifying the expression gives us the exact value for the linear speed:

$$v = \frac{15\pi}{45} = \frac{\pi}{3} \text{ m/s}$$

In an IB exam, you can provide the answer in exact form or as a decimal to 3 significant figures: $\approx 1.05 \text{ m/s}$.

parts.content: How far does a point on the rim travel in 5 minutes?
parts.markscheme:
- Convert 5 minutes to seconds: $5 \times 60 = 300$ seconds <marks>A1</marks>

- Recall formula: $\text{distance} = \text{speed} \times \text{time}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute values: $\text{distance} = \frac{\pi}{3} \times 300$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Final answer: $\text{distance} = 100\pi$ meters <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Convert time to seconds
steps.content:
To calculate the total distance, we first need to ensure our time units are consistent. The Ferris wheel's speed is given in seconds, so let's convert the travel time of 5 minutes into seconds.

$$5 \text{ minutes} \times 60 \text{ seconds/minute} = 300 \text{ seconds}$$

ti84.instructions: Type 5 * 60 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Convert minutes to seconds.

steps.title: Calculate number of revolutions
steps.content:
Next, we find out how many full or partial turns the wheel makes in 300 seconds. Since it takes 90 seconds for one revolution, we divide the total time by the time per revolution:

$$\text{Number of revolutions} = \frac{300 \text{ s}}{90 \text{ s}} = \frac{10}{3} \text{ revolutions}$$

ti84.instructions: Type 300 / 90 and press ENTER. To see it as a fraction, press MATH, then 1:Frac, then ENTER.

calculator.description: Divide total time by time per revolution.

highlights.text: completes one full revolution in 90 seconds

steps.title: Find distance per revolution
steps.content:
The distance traveled in one revolution is equal to the circumference ($C$) of the wheel. Using the radius of 15 metres and the formula for circumference:

$$C = 2\pi r$$
$$C = 2\pi(15) = 30\pi \text{ metres}$$

ti84.instructions: Type 2 * π * 15 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 2 * pi * 15 in the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the circumference.

steps.title: Calculate total distance
steps.content:
Finally, we multiply the number of revolutions by the distance per revolution to find the total distance traveled:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Total Distance} &= \text{Number of Revolutions} \times \text{Circumference} \\ &= \frac{10}{3} \times 30\pi \\ &= 100\pi \text{ metres} \end{aligned}$$

In decimal form, this is approximately $314$ metres.

ti84.instructions: Type (10 / 3) * 30 * π and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type (10 / 3) * 30 * pi in the expression bar.

calculator.description: Multiply revolutions by circumference.

steps.title: Convert time to seconds
steps.content:
To ensure our units are consistent with the rotation period (which is given in seconds), we first convert the total travel time from minutes to seconds.

$$t = 5 \times 60 = 300 \text{ seconds}$$

steps.title: Calculate the linear speed
steps.content:
The linear speed ($v$) of a point on the rim is the distance it travels in one full revolution (the circumference) divided by the time it takes for that revolution (the period).

First, we find the circumference ($C$):
$$C = 2\pi r = 2\pi(15) = 30\pi \text{ metres}$$

Next, divide by the period of $90$ seconds to find the speed:
$$\text{speed} = \frac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}} = \frac{30\pi}{90} = \frac{\pi}{3} \text{ m/s}$$

steps.title: Find the total distance
steps.content:
Now we multiply the constant linear speed by the total time elapsed to find the total distance traveled by the point.

$$\text{distance} = \text{speed} \times \text{time}$$
$$\text{distance} = \frac{\pi}{3} \times 300$$

Calculating the result:
$$\text{distance} = 100\pi \text{ metres}$$

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [^] to enter π. Then type [/] 3 [*] 300 and press [ENTER].

calculator.description: Calculate the final distance to verify the result.

steps.title: Convert time to seconds
steps.content:
To calculate how far the point travels, we first need to know the total time of travel in seconds, since the Ferris wheel's rotation rate is given in seconds.

$$5 \text{ minutes} = 5 \times 60 = 300 \text{ seconds}$$

steps.title: Calculate total angular displacement
steps.content:
Next, we find the total angle ($\theta$) the wheel turns in radians. We know one full revolution is $2\pi$ radians and takes 90 seconds. 

First, find the number of revolutions:
$$\text{Revolutions} = \frac{300 \text{ s}}{90 \text{ s}} = \frac{10}{3} \text{ revolutions}$$

Now, convert this to radians:
$$\theta = \frac{10}{3} \times 2\pi = \frac{20\pi}{3} \text{ radians}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 300 / 90 * 2 * π

calculator.description: Calculate the total angular displacement in radians.

highlights.text: completes one full revolution in 90 seconds

steps.title: Apply arc length formula
steps.content:
The distance travelled by a point on the rim is the arc length ($s$). Using the formula for arc length in radians:

$$s = r\theta$$

Substituting the radius $r = 15$ m and the angle $\theta = \frac{20\pi}{3}$:

$$s = 15 \times \left( \frac{20\pi}{3} \right)$$ 

This formula is covered in **AHL Topic 3.7: Radians**. If you are more familiar with degrees, remember that using radians often makes circular motion problems much simpler!

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Finally, simplify the expression to find the total distance:

$$\begin{aligned} s &= \frac{15}{3} \times 20\pi \\ s &= 5 \times 20\pi \\ s &= 100\pi \text{ metres} \end{aligned}$$

In an IB exam, you can leave your answer in terms of $\pi$ unless the question asks for a specific number of decimal places or significant figures. If calculated as a decimal, this is approximately $314$ metres.

parts.content: Let $s = \sqrt{3} + \mathrm{i}$. Plot the position of $s$ on an Argand diagram.
parts.markscheme:
- Correct position of $s$ in the first quadrant at $(\sqrt{3}, 1)$ <marks>A1</marks>

![Argand diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-faf09d8f-6fb2-4de9-a766-dc9700217696-1771929956947/c2e2c59d-0bad-4117-a8eb-e0ea44b258d5.jpeg)
1 mark total

parts.content: Express $s$ in the form $s = a\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i}b}$, where $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$, giving the exact value of $a$ and the exact value of $b$.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate modulus: $a = \sqrt{(\sqrt{3})^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{3 + 1} = 2$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate argument: $b = \arctan\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) = \frac{\pi}{6}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- State final answer: $s = 2\mathrm{e}^{\frac{\mathrm{i}\pi}{6}}$

2 marks total

<note>Accept equivalent exact arguments like $30^\circ$ only if converted properly to radians in later parts. Do NOT accept rounded decimal answers.</note>

parts.content: Let $w = s \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i}kt}$, where $k \in \mathbb{R}$. Express $w$ in the form $r\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i}(kt + \theta)}$, giving $r$ and $\theta$ as exact values.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute exponential form of $s$: $w = 2\mathrm{e}^{\frac{\mathrm{i}\pi}{6}} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i}kt}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Use properties of exponents to add arguments: $w = 2\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i}(kt + \frac{\pi}{6})}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Hence find $\mathrm{Im}(w)$ in the form $A \sin(kt + \alpha)$, where $A > 0$ and $0 < \alpha \le \pi/2$.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognize that $\mathrm{Im}(r\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i}\phi}) = r\sin\phi$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Apply to the expression for $w$: $\mathrm{Im}(2\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i}(kt + \frac{\pi}{6})}) = 2\sin(kt + \frac{\pi}{6})$ <marks>A1</marks>
- State values: $A = 2$ and $\alpha = \frac{\pi}{6}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content:
A mechanical system is subject to two harmonic force components, $F_1$ and $F_2$, both oscillating at an angular frequency of $20\text{ rad s}^{-1}$. The parameters for each component are shown in the following table:

| Force component | Maximum magnitude | Phase shift relative to $\sin(20t)$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $F_1$ | $1$ newton | $\frac{\pi}{2}$ |
| $F_2$ | $\sqrt{3}$ newtons | $0$ |

When both components act together, the total force $F_T$ is given by $F_1 + F_2$. Note that $F_T = 1\sin(20t + \frac{\pi}{2}) + \sqrt{3}\sin(20t)$.

Find the maximum magnitude of $F_T$.
parts.markscheme:
- Relate $F_T$ to the imaginary part of a complex product: $F_T = \cos(20t) + \sqrt{3}\sin(20t) = \mathrm{Im}((\sqrt{3} + \mathrm{i})\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i}20t})$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Use the result from part (d) with $k = 20$: $F_T = 2\sin(20t + \frac{\pi}{6})$ <marks>M1</marks>
- State the maximum value: $\text{Max magnitude} = 2$ newtons <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Find the smallest positive value of $t$ for which the force $F_T$ reaches its maximum magnitude.
parts.markscheme:
- Set the argument of the sine function to $\frac{\pi}{2}$: $20t + \frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{\pi}{2}$
- Solve for $t$: $20t = \frac{\pi}{3} \implies t = \frac{\pi}{60} \approx 0.0524$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

<note>Accept any equivalent correct form or rounded decimal to 3 s.f.</note>
